reviews.review_text: What a game. Never has the 40K universe been represented with such love and attention to detail. Highly recommend to both 40K fans and CRPG fans.

76 Hours, and it's over. And what a journey it was.

I'm a big 40k fan and I'm a big CRPG fan, esp those with a lot of narrative choices and such. This game was exactly what I was hoping, and expecting, and as such, I had an utter blast with it.

The story was a lot of fun with plenty of enjoyable twists and turns, the companions (base game at least) were fantastic. Some were better than others, but they all had a lot of character to them and they were an utter lovely bunch and extremely well written. I ended up misty eyed by the end LOL. 

The game very much understands the 40k setting. It understands, unlike Mr James Workshop, it's NOT about how badass and awesome space marines are. It understands it ISN'T about "le epic humanity." It understands the universe is a deeply insane, morbid, yet almost hilariously dark place to live in. It balances the line of "badass", "stupid as hell", "grimdark as hell", and "actually kind of nice" incredibly well. I would honestly go so far to say out of most 40k media I've read/played/watched, this gets it the most. It is extremely multifaceted in all the best ways, and understands it perfectly. I absolutely adore it for that, and I think if you're a 40k fan who gets frustrated by all the, y'know, nazis, in the community, you'll appreciate it. 

Now all that being said, it isn't a perfect game. There's some areas that were a bit iffy.

For starters, combat, your mileage will vary. I personally thought it was fine, a bit dense and confusing, but I'm an idiot and others haven't had that problem like I have. I thought it was fun, but had to turn it down to easy more than a few times. But I'm perfectly willing to say: Skill Issue. However... there's companions you recruit later on and MANY people have complained how they're built like absolute trash. And unless you have a godsend mod (more on that later) you have to lose very important resources to fix it, which is really rough.

Secondly, there's a lot of information thrown at you such as with the colony system, the trading stuff and reputation, and it can be a bit overwhelming. It certainly was for me. By the end of the game, I fumbled it really hard and felt really let down, because I barely got anything out of it. On a second playthrough I'll obviously do better, but that bummed me out a bit. But maybe it's on me for not being rogue trader-y enough, which I'll gladly accept if so.

Next... some of the companion quests were a bit lackluster compared to others? Some were super long, really in-depth, had a lot of great moments. Others felt... really short and a bit of a let down in comparison. I still enjoyed all the companions, but that felt pretty odd. And I'm not even going to touch on the "heretic" path. If you're expecting to be cunning and sneaky and evil, well... Put those hopes away. Iconoclast for the win anyway :P That'll make more sense if/when you play the game.


Next... the romances were... confusing. I thought I was going along for two, but they both never went anywhere and I'm really not sure where I went wrong. I 100% STARTED one but it didn't go anywhere. I know I'm not the only one, and it doesn't help in trying to call someone creepy, I apparently started a relationship with them?? Its a bit confusing, but hey, that's what guides are for.

Next, there were a few moments in gameplay that quite frankly, sucked ass. Such as a terrible designed stealth section in a game that is NOT built for stealth. Why do devs love this? Then another section that was a maze with INCREDIBLY long loading screens that were deeply frustrating to me and many other people. Some quests were still bugged me for, which was a let down too, and I know it's happened to others too. Not enough to ruin the whole experience, but a bit of a frustrating time on otherwise very great game. 

But... y'know what? Even with all of that, even with all my gripes, I absolutely loved my experience with this. Like I said: It was EXACTLY what I hoped for and expected. A solid, fun, well-written 40k crpg. And that's exactly what you get. It fills the niche I wanted it to. If you like 40k and you like CRPGs, you will 100% enjoy your time with this. I look forward to getting the DLCs at some point and doing a second playthrough. I also VERY much look forward to Dark Heresy, too.

Anyway, download toybox. Trust me. Just do it. You won't regret it. Incredibly useful mod to have.
steam deck users beware: only menu and conversation text is legible. interaction text is blurry to unplayability.

In the grim darkness of the far future, you find yourself as an heir to an ancient Rogue Trader bloodline. Although events beyond your control have placed you on the von Valancius throne and granted you a vast inheritance, you still have much to do to prove your worth in the wild west of the galaxy. 

There are rough edges in Rogue Trader. The game starts slow, and in my opinion, it doesn't do a perfect job of explaining its core mechanics. However, you gradually master them through trial and error. Don't be afraid to make mistakes with your character abilities as you can change them later on. On the other hand, companion interactions can drastically alter how that character’s story ends, even if you don't realize it at the time of your decision. Also, if you haven’t played any CRPGs other than Baldur's Gate 3 before, you might find the voice acting department a bit lacking. That being said, these are the only negative things I can say about the game. It is incredibly welcoming for newcomers to the Warhammer 40,000 universe. The characters, quests, and locations are engaging enough. I didn't encounter any game-breaking bugs, but I did get stuck a few times and had to look up guides online. The two DLCs I've played so far, Void Shadows and Lex Imperialis, were well worth the purchase, and I'm looking forward to the upcoming DLCs.

I am a recent convert to the CRPG genre and have enjoyed Rogue Trader tremendously. The game is simple enough where you can read the abilities without much digging and create a powerful, fun, build. No guides needed. The Story is excellent. It is a touch too long but held my attention.

Great RPG, especially if you're a 40k fan. Fight Drukhari in Commorough, Necrons, warp demons, cultists, chaos marines, defilers, and a ton of other enemies. Ground combat, space combat, diplomacy, economics. Chainswords, bolters, meltas, flamers, etc., it's all there. DLC is great, too.

Loved Pathfinder and played over 2000 hours Warhammer 3 Immporal Empires, was spectical about this one as I wasnt familiar with 40k universe. Boy, I was wrong, great game, love character progress, building op characters, blood and gore, choices matter. Wish it was fully voiced, but I understand it is ♥♥♥♥ ton of dialog to voice, extra cost.

PSA: Dont initiate the Solomorne Ablutions event twice! it will delete all of his equipped items.


weird bugs aside, still a 10/10 experience.

Very fun gameplay but very slow game.i went to meet with the mayor of a planet and it took me 7 hours lol.the environments are not very lively,like i went to a town but theres nothing what human environment will have.good game though, very fun. 7.5/10. If you like crpg definitely try divinity 2. A game that made my 150 hours pass like butter.
A great isometric RPG in a very engaging setting. Proper turn-based RPGs are rare and this is a fantastic addition. Very much recommended.

Просто изумительно. Лучшая игра в плане раскрытия мира Вархаммер 40к. Жаль, что не вытащили на уровень остальных веток прохождение за еретика (в плане реакции мира на решения, было бы здорово, если бы наши компаньоны отворачивались от нас из-за убеждений). За второе прохождение можно обнаружить массу сюрпризов. ДЛС прикольные, контента всё больше со временем. Спасибо разработчикам, Император Защищает.
New to WH but very fun and not overly confusing. Enjoyable and gratifying combat

A really deep CRPG with loads of builds and plenty of content to use them in.
This game got me into warhammer.

A CRPG for 40k fans and inductees alike. The game is dense and filled with possibility. The stories great, the characters solid, so solid that I have FOMO about leaving someone behind. The lore is dense, and being its one of my favorite franchises in existence, I'm eating and adoring every minute of it. The game does a lot right, and I have little to complain about.

My favorite 40k game.
One of the best representations of the universe and very close to what comes to my mind when reading the books.

I don't really know why I don't enjoy this game much, given I've played stuff like Pillars of Eternity, BG3, and Divinity 2. It just feels lacking overall in a bunch of different areas that ends up in a meh experience overall.
Story isn't engaging me enough to want to keep going, gameplay is slow and stale without much to change it up, character/gear/skill progression barely does anything, ect.
Flying around and exploring planets sounds cool, until you realize it's just the same problem as early No Man's Sky; wide as an ocean and deep as a puddle. You get like random rewards hovering over places and maybe a small map to actually walk around and do things. The whole managing different colonies and all that is also just really disappointing as all that I interact with it is via buttons and getting feedback only in static rewards that could be powercrept or basically useless.
Actual player choices and agency just... don't really do anything? Basically nothing happens, I dunno.
At this point I'd rather replay BG3/Divinity 2 than finish this game.

Everything RPG wise is great except class building and levelling is insanely confusing.  but the actual gameplay like combat? so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ tedious and long, youll find yourself actually dreading getting into a fight that isnt challenging or difficult and just a long grindy fight against damage sponges

Chapter 3 is not fun.

The game does not allow for a truly dogmatic playthrough. Potentially you can get there with a certain builds (maybe) but ultimately the game forces all players onto Commorragh regardless of game decisions made by the player. Where your choice is to beg (on your knees) a Xeno for help or offer to work for said alien. Anything else leads to game over. - Note that these actions are not considered heretical (why....the plot needs it....) and you can technically still complete a 100% dogmatic play through.

This is an issue as the game offers different content based on play style Iconoclast/Dogmatic/Heretical. I wanted to roleplay a 100% Dogmatic Rogue Trader. I killed potential and existing party members for heresy, knowing it would lock me out of game content. The character I am roleplaying would not do either of the options available, and would prefer to die (they fear the corruption of their soul) than commit such blatant heresy.

On top of this, the gameplay of act 3 is sub par. The game has essentially been teaching you about building and managing a space empire, and just as things look interesting, and a space war looks likely to erupt, you are forced into what the internet claims is a 4 - 7 hour dungeon crawl. - I wouldn't know, I uninstalled when I realised the story I was playing made no sense. I lost all faith in the devs, as this is clearly such poor gameplay and story telling. I genuinely don't get it, all I think, is that someone had a ''great idea'' but lacked the ability to pull off said idea.

This is all such a shame. I was genuinely enjoying this game. I thought I have found a hidden gem. I was looking forwards to doing Iconoclast and Heretical runs but the story has been ruined. Knowing Commorragh is a thing that can't be skipped is also gruelling. I can see why so many people give this game a good review. As a game, it is good but nothing special. Asides from the story, which is the main appeal of the game.... and the story shat the bad.... so the is game rubbish and I can not recommend it for your time :(

This would be a great game except for three things: 

1.   Random non-avoidable chapter that is well, not what I bought the game to play.  Boring, tedious, and a lot of looking things up on YouTube to work out how to get out of the totally unrealistic 40k scenario that, in 40k should read "game over". 

2.   Rediculous unbalanced mechanical bosses.   Come on Owlcat, enough with the "this monters gets to shoot after every one of your characters takes a turn, and auto-heals as much HP as your party has every round even if you break all the batteries."   Its ... rediculously bad design.   Exactly like a GM saying "I ran out of ideas, I know, I will give this level 6 party Tiamat's daughter as a boss encounter".   Its just ... lazy and bad.

3.   Travel is tedious and boring, and 100 hours in I cannot for the life of me think why they have a colony management game layer, it just seems like a make-work not a proper element of the game.  Particularly as that is totally irrelvant when you need it, i.e. (1) and (2) above.

I do like Owlcat games, but to be candid, other people make better story focused games.   These guys feel like they are trying to put too much in, to the point where they forgot its meant to be a fun game.   There is nothing quite like your tech 100 Techpriest not being able to open computer... ummm... guys?

I don't know if the game gets better, but the fact the concsensus advice seems to be rebuild your characters to use every cheesy exploit in the game to max DPS and have the best tank possible is the only way to defeat certain bosses to advance the story ... it's not looking good.

This game takes a minute to get into and that threshold can be daunting but once you get into it, this game is amazing. The depth is staggering, combat feels balanced and challenging but not rage inducing. I recommend this game to anyone into CRPGs

Awesome setting/world spoiled by frustrating gameplay decisions. 
Pacing feels off ( Endless talking followed by endless combat). 
Leveling up is not just overwhelming the players, but also the devs, leading to completely busted builds/fights. Here just one example of 100s of abilities you have to choose from: "The Assassin attacks and grants +40% dodge to the target for this attack. If this attack hits, it deals an additional +(lethality / 5) (rounded down) damage increased by twice the percentage of that target's missing wounds"... WTF... 
I quit after getting stuck in a maze glued together by an endless amount of  loading screens.

Honestly this is just a good crpg. I loved this game. Excellent world, excellent game. I am a big fan of CRPGs and have historically had a fairly minimal interest in Warhammer, having found the law interesting but not having a genere of game that really clicked for me personally. This game's sheer amount of systems, good story telling and great crpg. This is honestly now one of my favoritue CRPGs. I'm off to become weirdly invested in the Warhammer unvierse now. Thanks Owlcat!
Very dark and interesting world. It's the first time experiencing it for me. It's a great game as expected for Owlcat Games.

:SOME SPOILERS:

Decent CRPG until the Dark Elf part.  Getting "abducted" with no way to fight back and getting all your characters perma-debuffed in some cases all because of some very "shoved in your face" character alteration-narration.  Even the "escape" is stupid and contrived and the only way they could think of getting a Space Marine on a Rogue Trader crew.  Not to mention the final act which I just had to abandon because after a few quests everything became a game of 'fix all these stupid things that came about after you got a vacation to dark eld town'.  Which becomes tiresome very fast and made me switch to games that don't railroad my experience.  And it was heartbreaking because I had a ton of time played in the beginning stuff.  Most of the classes are fine but a little too in your face with the CRPG elements.  Like the  whole zone setup for some classes is just plain stupid when you consider how often your character need to move out of those zones.  That "trader" companion is on the shelves for good because I can't be bothered to deal with her class combinations.  On the other hand, the addon companion "death dancer girl" is built for a combination that just breaks the game.  She moves so far and attacks so much each round that with even basic gear you can kill every enemy in an encounter.  And that is not hyperbole.  Ranged characters are ok but get action capped like crazy.  But if you build a melee assassin you can move farther than ranged characters can shoot and can AOE attack about 3-12 times a round depending on level.  Just flipping around AOE bleeding/poison/10,000 other debuffs added with each AOE attack.  Nothing like shooting two enemies for 50% of their health and feeling good about how far your character has come only for some rando NPC girl to lay out everyone and their mother.  Dev's obviously played Tyranids on board because only melee gets any love.  Play Tau?  Congrats, your fusion rounds have been replaced with silly string.

Then casters, sorry, psykers.  Very standard CRPG fare, you can't use most of your abilities more than two times before you are pretty much forced back to shooting things poorly.  Or being insane enough to try and be a melee psyker.  There is a reason the companion inquisitor gets wounds almost every round on harder difficulties.  His force fed character build is why inquistors need a large retinue I guess, they just get stomped out if someone looks at them funny.  Old Navy man is fine as a tar pit tank (until dark elf time, he gets really perma-screwed up there and is better of getting shelved for someone not ruined).  Probably the best advice is if you really want to play make sure to look up for when in game you are about to get abducted by dark elves and then fill up your party with the NPC's you really don't care about or use.  Unless that's impossible, I forget if everyone gets abducted or just your current members.

In fact I just Googled how to skip Chapter 3 and you only can with Mods.  Also, you can only Mod out the stupid cripple/injury stuff from your abduction.  Honestly Cyberpunk, this game, any game where your character gets a crippling injury that will screw you over and you can't get rid of isn't appealing.  That's real life kinda ♥♥♥♥ that isn't fun or enjoyable for anyone when they realize that the writing is on the wall so to speak.

So if you really want a CRPG  to spend some time on then go for it if it's on sale.  But otherwise you'll have a better time with Space Marine 2 for your 40k fix and Wasteland 2/3 or BG3 for a good story CRPG.
This game is so atmospheric. It sucks you in and keeps your attention the whole time. If you love 40k you'll adore this.

8/10
Great rpg with lots to love about it.
Runs fine, sounds great, looks good.
Would have preferred a bit fewer loading screens and more details on models.

I've been playing D&D type CRPGs for decades and I love that style of gaming. I'm new to Warhammer 40K and wasn't sure what to expect with no real understanding of the franchise.  I'm not to far into the game yet, but i am hooked!  Very happy with the purchase.  

Having read other peoples posts, I saw that only the Prologue is fully voiced and not the rest of the game.  So I did some searching and found a modder who did a great job leveraging a Text to Speech software tool so now you can have voiced dialog post-prologue.  You can fins the Mod here -> https://www.nexusmods.com/warhammer40kroguetrader/mods/431?tab=description

Rogue Trader is first and only game to flesh out the 40k universe and give you a sense of it on a larger scale.  Games like the space marine franchise, dark tide, mechanicus, etc.. do ok at portraying a very narrow field of view from a single perspective, but they're limited to that.

Memorable companions, weighty decisions with lasting effects, great story and incredibly in depth character build system and a sense of adventure.  So far it's my favorite 40k game by miles.

I've been wanting to play this game for years but it simply will not let me play beyond character creation. I have tried every single suggested fix and it simply will not work.

Any time I have spent on this game has either been in character creation, settings or trying to move past character creation.

There's clearly a lot of attention to detail in this game and maybe big 40k fans will love it, but I just found it tedious after a while. Every level-up felt like a massive break in pacing while I went through a chorelist of abilities with all these different effects and nuances only for the gameplay with these abilities to boil down to: A. Shoot enemy B. Run at enemy and hit them. 

Apparently making the titular rogue trader a melee build is a terrible idea because before I even had a full party I ended up with three melee guys on my team that did essentially the same job. I tried to distinguish them a little bit (damage vs tankiness and whatnot), but in the end they all do the same thing.

And the game acts like all the loot thrown at me means something, but aside from a few weapons and armor that I could use to kit up my party, I have literally no idea what any of it is for. I expect that it eventually plays into an economic/management gameplay side that I was actually looking forward to, but after 12.5 hours of boring ass combat, I'm throwing in the towel. 

The relative you inherit the rogue trader title from seemed like this aloof, all-powerful demigod that ruled her crew with an iron fist, but your bumass is instead forced to walk around the planet with everyone else and chase down tiny bits of loot while struggling to deal with normal bandits. There's no sense of power, no satisfaction in the combat, and no break from the monotony of reading text box after text box after text box. Most of them aren't even voice-acted, which I feel should be the bare minimum for a character-drive rpg.

Also the romance aspect came out of nowhere and felt incredibly creepy in the context of the game. Seems like loserbait to me.

Pros: very fun 40k party crpg, yay

Cons: bad dlc practices, promise 3 dlc in a season pass, deliver 2 dlc and release second dlc pass, boo

I guess I would recommend.  However it isn't 100%.  I haven't finished the game but my gripe is kind of with the beginning.  This is not owlcats fault.  Rather it is with the ttrpg that I think they are using.  Unless owlcat made the ttrpg themselves.  In the beginning the complexity of the builds leaves something to be desired.  If I remember correctly there are 3 classes.  Eventually those become more classes and it seems like in the third phase only one class in the end.  Kind of like fire emblem in that way but not.

So they use a feat system but a lot of the feats seem like something of situational.  I couldn't bring myself to play it in the beginning.  The combat seemed kind of like so many of the other games that are tactical like this which is simplistic and lifeless.  Eventually though it picks up though I will say.  About around level 7 or so.  Psykers are the closest thing to a magic system in this game and they don't really compare to pathfinder.  Also later on they punish you for going with the only interesting origin or whatever you call it.  Just randomly instantly gibs my psyker main character because of a bad roll.

Anyway those kind of in a garbled sense are my misgivings about this game.  It is getting better.  The combat... I don't know I don't like the combat.  The writing is a little bit better.  The music is decent though I don't remember specific soundtracks like in the 2nd level of the mite caves in kingmaker.  Despite what people are thinking I just am hard to please in this way.  This is still better than a lot of the other games that are crpg.  I would recommend it if you find that you don't need intellectual stimulation as much as I do.  Again that probably isn't owlcats fault but the ttrpg is almost too simple for me.

Which the intellectual stimulation is weird because I find that I'm more and more attracted to reading books lately.  Good books on complex subject manner mind you.  Also sci fi or space fantasy is different than my typical romp.  I am open to the experience having just entered into act 2.  I heard that act 4 falls off or something which is something that I'm not looking forward to.

I had a phase where I was much more into the 40k lore but not right now or I would be eating this up in a sense.  At phase 2 there seems to be like 4-6 possible classes or something I didn't count but they are based off of your first class.  Again if I were the ttrpg company I wouldn't change it.  Things just aren't my cup of tea.  I still will play it more which is more than I can say about some other turn based tactical games.  So it isn't terrible.  If they were to change it and make it more complex I think that they would get less people to play their game.  Maybe simplistic isn't the right word.  It is more like I want more than 3 classes of significance in the beginning.  I don't know completely where my disdain comes from.  The different characters seem kind of samey.  Not completely and it does get better later.  However I am repeating myself so I will end my verbose review here.

3 hours in so far and I really like what I see, can't wait to see what else is in store with the story and characters. My positive experience with the game was enough to pick up the DLC which is suppose to be really great also. If you like CRPG games like the pathfinder series you'll like this. For the Emperor!

Genuinely one of those games where I thought to myself "Wow... this game might actually not end!", and I mean that in the best way possible.

Easily one of the best 40K titles of all time. 

You don't even need to be familiar with the setting to enjoy it. Even if you don't know squat about 40K, the game explains everything to the point where you might end up being an expert yourself.
very good game if you are into crpg and story telling, this game is a gem with massive content and good stories.

Really enjoyable game so far besides too complicated RPG system. I guess halving the amount of different perks would serve the game much better than a current vastness of options. Also, RTWP absence is pitiful, as OwlCat likes to throw a lot of enemies at you, and at some point you're strong enough to let your team kill them without too much tactics and preparations involved.

Got into it a little late for someone who loves 40k, but glad I did as I really enjoyed my time with both of the DLC's. Must play for any 40k fan, even any CRPG fan, but especially if you understand the setting there's endless appreciation and "fan-service" albeit in the 40k way. Necron tomb world <<< 1 turn from Ulfar
Great game, some difficulty spikes in fights that are unfair (classic owlcat) but a good game none the less.

This is a amazing game! Does it have its flaws? Yeah of course it does but so does any other game. The graphics and animations aren't anything to write home about at least when comparing it to some other big games like BG3 and its true that there is A LOT of reading but most gamers can over look those things if the game is genuinely good like Rogue Trader is. As a major 40k nerd it doesn't get much better than this. Owlcat really knocked it out of the park with this one.

As I mentioned it does have a lot of reading involved with it because the game isn't fully voiced like other games nowadays but that is easily fixed with some great mods if that is an issue for you. If you love 40k this game is a must check out in my opinion.

Rogue Trader is probably the best 40k game released to date and a great roleplaying game in general. I finished my first playthrough of Rogue Trader last year, and it clocked at about one hundred hours, which included the Void Shadows dlc. And while it did have a few tedious bits, I enjoyed most of it. With a new dlc due to release in a couple of days, I figured I'd finally get around to writing a review. If you are familiar with older style crpgs, or even the more recent Baldur’s Gate 3, you’ll feel right at home, although Baldur’s Gate 3’s presentation is lightyears beyond of Rogue Trader’s. Where Rogue Trader shines is in its adaptation of the Warhammer 40k setting to the crpg format, a match that seems to be made in heaven. It puts you in the noble shoes, or military jackboots of a Rogue Trader, a member of a long line of explorers sent out by the Emperor of Mankind to expand the borders of the Imperium of Man. Think early modern explorers and colonizers. The border region featured in this game is an isolated region of space called the Koronus Expanse, where your dynasty and several others have made their domain. If you are unfamiliar with the lore, don’t worry, the game teaches you as play through the story. In the role of Rogue Trader you are tasked with exploring planets, governing colonies and dealing with threats to the Imperium and your own domain. How you go about doing this is entirely up to you. Are you a dogmatic adherent to the Imperial Creed, viewing your own life and the lives of your subjects as expendable resources to be used in the name of the God Emperor? Or do you embrace a more pragmatic or humane path, and try to create a better alternative in the Expanse to the oppressive regime that is the Imperium? Or do you embrace the path of heresy, and accept the gifts the dark gods of Chaos offer you? Rogue Trader excels in how you are able to play your character in this setting. Even though most of the story is relayed to you through text, which is sometimes voiced, the way you can influence the story, and the way the setting reacts to your choices really pulls you into the world.

Rogue Trader isn’t perfect. While most combat encounters are interesting puzzles, a few of the, mainly against weaker trash enemies, felt more like filler to expand game time. I didn’t actively dislike these combat encounters, and they do add to the feeling that you are in a larger conflict during some of the game’s more conflict-intense sections, but they don’t add much to the game. The character leveling UI also makes character creation and character building seem more complex than they actually are. The layout isn't great, since it just presents you with a long list of options. When I played the game around this time last year, there were a couple of trap talents in the game that were more or less a waste to take, such a talent that would let you wear power armor. I’ve read up on several updates that the game received since, and certain areas have been rebalanced to make these talents more useful. But for a first-time player I do recommend looking up an up-to-date guide to see what talents fit the character you want to make.

Compared to Owlcat’s previous games, the two Pathfinder games, there is less complexity, which makes the game a much better entry point to players unfamiliar with complex tabletop rulesets or players who are more interested in the story than optimized character building. As was included in Kingmaker and Wrath of the Righteous, Rogue Trader does feature an empire building mechanic in the form of colony management, although this mainly functions as a way to get interesting bonuses and gear and to have you explore optional areas of the map in search of resources.

What is interesting is that Rogue Trader doesn’t use a traditional rpg economic system, where you barter for goods with some form of currency. Because a Rogue Trader is an important noble of the Imperium, various factions will gift you items, depending on your standing with them and the profit factor of your domain. This is the main way you acquire gear, which is an interesting twist that works well from both a gameplay and narrative perspective.

One final critique I do have of the game is that the narrative can sometimes feel disjointed. The game dangles many threats in front of the player but doesn’t commit to exploring a single antagonist. The prologue and first act are all centered around Chaos, which makes it seem that they will be the central antagonistic force throughout the game. But in the other acts Chaos is relegated to the background, while other more diverse threats present themselves to the Rogue Trader. This does make it a great introduction to the 40k-setting and the many factions and forces within it, but it does feel less focused than other similar games. Don’t worry though, it all comes together in the end, it just takes a while to get there. The main driving forces throughout the story is the welfare of your dynasty and domain and finding out exactly what your predecessor, Theodora, was up to. And those are interesting enough threads to follow.

Overall, Rogue Trader captures what makes the 40k-universe so interesting and dark. And by putting you in it as a character that exists within the context of this setting, manages to create one of the most interesting crpg-experiences I’ve played. This is a really good game, and without a doubt the best Warhammer 40k I’ve played to date. And the game has an awesome soundtrack!

I cannot even begin to describe how much I've fallen in love with this game, I usually don't like CRPGs but my god is this game fantastic, while some points of the game can be a bit weak or annoying those points are quickly overshadowed by the gameplay and the very large assortment of builds, choices, and options you can do with your character and companions, you can play this game for hundreds of hours and have something different happen in each playthrough, I'm still on my first character and my god is this fun, I high recommend getting the game and the DLCs, cause this game is 1000% worth it, I cant wait till the 11th cause that the release of another DLC featuring one of the coolest necrons, big daddy collector. Trazyn the infinite, I dont even have a high end pc and the game runs pretty ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ well so once again Get this game trust me not a waste of your money, and as many in the fandom would say, FOR THE IMPERIUM, FOR HUMANITY, FOR THE GOD EMPEROR OF MANKIND RAHHHHHHHH!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

You don't see a whole lot of games with gut-wrenching decision making these days, many of them are linear or excessively open-ended. Warhammer 40k Rogue Trader forces you into corners with consequences you will never recover from, and the story is beyond immersive for someone like me who finds the world of warhammer so interesting. I find it hard to tear myself away from it to take of things in real life lol. If you already enjoy games with role-playing elements and turn based tactics combat, I *highly* recommend giving it a try.

Rogue Trader is honestly one of the best CRPGs I've played in a long time. When I think about what makes a great CRPG, the first things that come to mind are the writing, characters, worldbuilding, roleplaying, etc. Rogue Trader absolutely nails all of that. The writing is fantastic throughout. The dialogue feels rich and intelligent, the lore is incredibly deep, and the game does a great job making you feel like an actual Rogue Trader navigating a dangerous and complicated corner of the 40K universe. The companions were probably the biggest surprise for me. This might genuinely be one of my favorite RPG casts ever. Almost every character felt memorable, interesting, and fun to have around. There were very few points where I wasn't invested in what they had to say or where their personal stories were going. The combat was tactical, satisfying, and stayed engaging for most of the game. My biggest gameplay complaint is probably the leveling system. There's just so much stuff. Between all the talents, archetypes, and 55 levels worth of progression, leveling up eventually started feeling like homework. The depth is cool but I think it could have been streamlined without losing much. That being said, the writing is so good that it constantly made me wish the game had been fully voice acted. There are so many great conversations, character moments, and lore dumps that would've hit even harder if they had the production value to fully voice it. The companions in particular really should have had more of their lines voiced. Despite having a more modest budget and some of the limitations that come with it, I still found myself putting it in the same conversations as some of my favorite RPGs. If this game had the production value of a Baldur's Gate 3, I genuinely think it would be competing if not beating a lot of my top games. An exceptional CRPG with phenomenal writing, incredible companions, and meaningful roleplaying

8.9/10 (Not a game for Justin though)

TLDR: This is an Owlcat style CRPG game with the deep story, incredible world exploration  and fantastic character build options..... but also a very long game to finish.

I'm a fan of the Grimdark 40k universe despite never getting into the table top minatures. This game does a great job of capturing a universe with an Imperium that is raging against the dying light. Everything here feels both advanced and ancient at the same time. The characters are wonderfully written with a psychology matching the setting and not the 21th century west. 

What sets this game aside from other WH40k games is the role you play: you are a Rogue Trader. While space marines and mechanicus priests are awesome, you get to experience the world through the eyes of an 'ordinary' citizen who has been elevated to a position of great power. You set to see a side of this universe in a way that the other games do not cover.

Overall I strongly recommend this to players who love old school turn based CRPGs, are patient and willing to put in some serious hours into character design and team building. I also recommend it to WH40k fans who enjoy seeing their setting brought to life in front of them.

Blood for the God Emporer?
Skulls for the Golden Throne?
Mistreated peasants for the bowels of the ship?

Morals lost in the void?
Definitely a 40k game. A good one.
Incredible game, owlcat does it again. I was a huge fan of the Pathfinder games and this just takes that phenomenal framework to the WH40K universe. Really well done.

TL,DR
A game that pains me not to recommend, but I can’t do so for a game I have up on the 20th hour. In the end, I couldn’t get into the world, be it because of the source material, never was a grim dark fan, or from Owlcat’s style, but the consequence is not carrying about the narrative, something paramount to na RPG. Not even the very lack luster coop mode could save the experience, as your friend is relegated to a NPC, 
Story
If this is a good entry point to the 40k franchise, I do not wish to see a bad one. An important preface for this review is that I knew nothing about this world, but this game has not improved my situation. 
There are two core issues: worldbuilding and a lack of a call to action. Speaking at the 20h mark, I remain without the knowledge of how this world works or its structures, only vague definitions such as a Rogue trader having massive freedom on how deal with stuff in the world and how the warp is a destructive force. The game relies far too much into written definitions, with constant use of in universe words and codex entries, but lacks in the visual aspect of story telling, with scenes doing very little to set the universe. A perfect contrast  is Dragon Age Inquisition, a game I entered as the 3rd of the franchise, but by the end of the prologue could understand that the chantry is an analogue to the catholic church and that a peace summit between those who can use magic and those who control magic was blown up, leading to the chaos of the start of the game.
Important to note that this is not a limitation of the CRPG format, DOS2 manages to catch up the player on source, voidwoken and the concept of being godwoken quite quickly. The lacked of a voiced narrator, and the game bouncing between fully voiced, and not voiced at all hurts the execution the most, as one doesn’t focus as much on the written text. While there’s nothing wrong with a non voiced game, I must say that it doesn’t fit well with playing this game after a long day’s work. 
 This ties rather to the second problem, the lack of a call to action, why should I hunt Kunrad, why should I act on this world. The necessity of acting against the breach is clear in Inquisition, hunting for a cure to your tadpole is a clear goal in BG3 or the chip in Cyberpunk. This combination makes Rogue Trade a game difficult to get into if you already don’t like the the 40k universe. 
There are other smaller problems, thanks the lack of the plot gripping you, pacing is a problem. Characters are too archetypical, Cassia is a great example of this as the cloistered noblewoman, similar to Caitlyn Kiraman in Arcane, but while the later has a clear representation of her rage over this situation, joining the enforcers against her family’s wishes, nothing of such is present in Cassia on the 20hrs. Caitlyn is not the only way to make this arc, but Cassia has no clear cut direction on what they wish to do, she remains the innocent noblewoman. 

Gameplay
Combat is solid, but confusing. It relies heavily in the debuffs and passives, which would be fine if there weren’t so many of them. While better than pathfinder, builds remain obscure with the sheer amount of options overwhelming the player.
Difficulty is poorly scaled, at times normal is a cakewalk, while daring bounces between manageable and silly difficult. The leap between the two is simply too great for a new player, and while yes, one can fine tune with a custom difficulty, this shouldn’t be the player’s responsibility. 
I have no doubt that if one truly immerses themselves into this system, there’s plenty of enjoyment, fun and whacky builds to play with. But it simply requires a time I do not have. 
A comment about the coop, it borders on false advertisement. Your invitee will effectively take control of a NPC, tossed away from any interaction on the ships bridge or in any story moment.
Conclusion
I won’t deny there’s a good game here, but not one for me. A classic CRPG that was requires a dedication that I cannot place. Seems unfair to give 6/10 to this game, as I see it as a better game than Veilguard in most aspects, but only the latter I managed to finish.

Before playing Warhammer 40,000: Rogue Trader, I had my doubts. CRPGs are not always my type of game, even though I've always loved the Warhammer 40K universe. But once I started playing, I got completely addicted.
The story is absolutely fantastic, filled with unexpected twists and moments that genuinely caught me by surprise. It's the kind of narrative that stays with you long after you've finished playing. The characters, choices, and consequences make every decision feel important.
What really stands out is how well the game captures the dark, grim atmosphere of the Warhammer 40K universe. The horrors you encounter, the difficult moral choices, and the constant feeling that humanity is fighting against impossible odds make the experience unforgettable. The game embraces the darkness of the setting and delivers it perfectly.
Owlcat Games did an incredible job bringing this universe to life, and as a fan of Warhammer 40K, I couldn't have asked for more. I honestly can't wait for the next DLC and to dive back into this amazing adventure.
For the Emperor!

good game

It was great that I have spent a lot of time digesting the lore, the story, and the gameplay until I have encountered a game breaking bug in CHAPTER 3 that is unable to progress because the enemy AI spawns far outside the battle area. Load before the battle does nothing. Uninstalled and installed months later thinking that the bug would be fixed, nah. I was excited for the upcoming DLCs yet the thought of redoing the game is a big NO for me. Fix your bugs..... What a waste of my time!

I cannot express enough how much I love this game. It's got some minor quirks, like all games, but these are overall nitpicks the devs have done a fairly good job of addressing since the game's launch. Still, I'd like to quickly mention them, followed of course by everything this game does right.

First, the bad (minor grievances):

- Too many companions, and too many quests! Yes, really, that's my chief complaint. My steak is too juicy, my lobster too buttery. To get all the game's content, you need to do multiple runs. Not that that's a problem, the game has great replayability due to the alignment system giving you different choices and endings. That said, if you want to be like me, and experience all the companions plus their quests in one run because you want to be friends with everyone, it adds a lot of playtime. At the time of writing this review, note that I have done several runs, most lightly modded and uncompleted to explore different character, build, and story options. My completionist run with the first 2 DLCs took about 150-ish hours overall. I am also a relatively slow and meticulous gamer, I like leaving no corner of a map unexplored and no loot left untaken, and I admit I often reload if and when I ♥♥♥♥ up. Still, 120 hours is the time I've seen thrown around by some others, and subsequent runs go by quicker once you know the system. It took me several unfinished runs of trial and error to choose a good build and story path I liked. Speaking of...

- The character progression system is a minor nightmare that takes a lot of getting used to. You know the meme about all games being either parkour or menus? Yeah, so this, in line with many other cRPGs, is the final boss of menus. There are literally almost too many talents, weapons, armors, etc. and the process of leveling up takes forever. This time is compounded when you have decided that you want all the companions in your party at once all the way to the end of the game. You start to lose track of which talents you've taken for what character, and what they all do, and you will spend ages agonizing over which new ones to take. The same goes for equipment and who has what. You are literally constantly finding armor and weapons, many of which are unique, but most of which are trash and should go straight to cargo, but if you're a hoarder like me you won't want to move most of it to cargo "just in case" you need it later (you will never in fact need it, sorry) which makes it difficult to sort through. I would recommend looking up build guides to follow, as you may get lost very easily without them.

- The combat is too easy. After Act 1, if you're playing on Daring or easier, you can expect almost every combat to only last a single round because your party will be able to annihilate everything in your path immediately if you build them right. If you want a challenge, you either need to go with custom difficulty to at least turn down momentum gain, or go up to Hard or Unfair.

- Related to the last point, the initiative system is pretty busted. As in, it just doesn't really work well or make sense. You get big clumps of enemies spaced out by maybe one or two characters, with the rest of your party all bunched up at the end.

- Lack of male/male romance options. Sorry, there's just no getting around this one. Your options are an evil twink elf who's into BDSM and murder, or a cop who is only available with the Lex Imperialis DLC. The female romances are amazing, however, for both male and female player characters.

Now, on to the good (shorter section to avoid spoilers):

- The writing is phenomenal, peak 40k. I loved all the characters, and the story was overall very good. I have to admit I cried a little when I reached the ending slides. Acts 4 and 5 felt slightly rushed, but the 3rd and 4th DLCs should help flesh out the last parts of the game a bit more (and probably add another 15-30+ hours of playtime between them). I have no complaints, massive kudos to Owlcat's writing team for all the work they did. The game is basically a novel.

- The 40k lore is integrated seamlessly without overexplaining concepts not relevant to the events of the game, and it does an incredible job of really drawing you into the setting. The atmosphere is perfect, and the music is phenomenal.

- The voice acting is incredible, and my only wish is that the entire game was fully voiced. Still, with the massive amount of dialogue and branching story paths, I understand why it couldn't have been.

- The character creation is a ton of fun, you can lose several hours just playing around with different options to design the perfect Rogue Trader, it's extremely customizable. You can also recruit mercenaries by talking to Janris Danrock, which allows you to create a second custom character. Honestly though, I don't usually use this feature, because I love the companions all so much I can't just leave them out.

- Abelard, he is the best. And he introduces me, which is a valuable service I simply cannot go without.

Overall... please buy this game. Despite the nitpicks, it is a truly incredible and unique experience, and can be a good introduction to the 40k universe even if you go in knowing nothing about it!

Deep deep game. Sometimes I skip through dialogue and then have no idea what's going on. It's fun though, and the role-playing elements are strong. 

I am not a huge Warhammer enthusiast, but this game has so much lore that I'm beginning to understand what makes the universe so interesting. I'm hooked.

Honestly, just wait for the game to be actually finished with the rest of DLCs. I loved the start of the game, the ship feels alive, your companions are interacting with you and the story, you keep discovering things about the setting and the plot and... act 3 happens, it is kind off limited and railroady but it kind of makes sense. You finish it and continue to act 4 and then it starts to fall apart, you start to see worlds that are just combat and no missions, there is no interactivity between companions. You might gun down a whole inquisition squad and the inquisitor adyacent companion that is with you will not even utter a word.

The whole thing seems to stagnate and that takes out all the fun. The end of act 5 is also extremely rail roady, why should I chase this particular person? Why should I leave my domains? Why do I even care? 

I will not critique the combat even though it is still unbalanced, but sure, maybe I could have gone up to unfair and things would have been better, even though I think it also affects your skill checks.

The later chapters are made worse because of how nice the first ones are. It seems obvious that at some point they ran out of budget or time, and for me at least the easiest way to see it is that in every planet in the first few acts where you drop off, you get your little dropship guarded by two rifleman, you have your own pilot and a tech priest doing tech priesty things to the ship. That stops being a thing. A lack of polish, for some reason it makes me thing of 2005 farenheit, with the lovely dinner scene that starts the game, and the ending which is just rushed bs.
You dont need extensive understanding of 40K to play. it's just a cool game for cool people.

the good: the writing in this game is delightful. 40k shines best when it's kind of a parody of both itself and the grimdark genre as a whole, and this game has cleverness leaking out of every pore. its so so fun to be a petty, unpredictable dictator of a shambling nightmare bureaucracy and i think everybody should give it a shot.

Made with love and respect for the material. Story and atmosphere are absolutely fantastic. Combat is challenging (turn up the difficulty to at least Daring!) and a lot of fun, especially with the many crunchy weapon effects.

Some minor criticisms: this is one of the few games where leveling up felt like a chore. The system is not simple and requires thinking, and the leveling up UI could help a bit more. Second criticism is that the camera tends to be covered a lot of the time by vertical parts of scenery, so one tends to adjust the camera very often. I don't remember that from other isometric RPGs.

This game has some highs, but a lot of it is just kind of boring? Levelling your characters is really very tedious, and combat doesn't feel like it has much weight to it. The story beats are strong, the dialogue great, and the choices terrific, but everything surrounding it doesn't live up to what it promises.

At a point, when booting it up feels like a chore, I stopped playing.

I love the passion that OwlCat put into this game. This is possibly the first game I'm certain of putting in multiple play throughs. The turn-based combat is engaging and nail-biting once conflict ramps up. I love the wound system because it adds another layer of worry and consideration when going through an area. The characters are fun and I am enjoying seeing their stories unravel.

A must play if you are a fan of Warhammer 40K. One of these games where you can get lost for hours engrossed in the lore. This is also one of these games where the best of your intentions can wind up having the worst of all outcomes and I will just leave it at that. Game has many parallels on what is going on in the current world as of 2026. Would most definitely play for a second run sometimes in the future.  By the way this is not a casual play type game. You can thank Tales from the Void on YouTube for partially influencing me to buy this game. Yes, all the reviews you hear on how this game is like reading a whole novel is definitely true. I do kind of feel if they are giving you lore heavy conversational dumps this should have be voice acted. But on the other hand I can understand why they are not going to voice act every line in game. Overall worth a buy only if you are big fan of the Warhammer 40K Lore.
